title: Azure DevOps hierarchy, area paths, and scoping containers
topic: Azure DevOps work item hierarchy modeling: how does Azure DevOps structure Epic → Feature → User Story → Task → Bug? Cover (1) the schema/data model — single work_items table with a Work Item Type field, or per-type tables, and how parent-child links are stored (links table vs. parent_id field); (2) the strategy↔execution boundary — does Azure separate portfolio/strategic items from execution items, and where is the seam; (3) parent-child link rules — does a child have one parent or many, are tree links a separate "Parent" link type from other link types ("Related", "Predecessor", "Tested By", etc.); (4) rollup mechanics — how does parent rollup work for status, completion, story points, remaining work, and is it trigger-maintained, computed-on-read, or computed by Analytics views; (5) reparenting — can a User Story move between Features cleanly, what happens to historical reporting and rollups; (6) orphan handling — what's the UX/process for a work item with no parent, is it allowed, warned, or blocked; (7) subtree filtering — Boards/Backlogs/Queries — how does a user say "show me everything under this Feature" and is it via tree query, ancestor link, or area path; (8) custom level naming — Agile/Scrum/CMMI/Basic process templates differ on hierarchy levels; can tenants customize work item types, add levels, or rename Epic/Feature without breaking inheritance; (9) Area Paths and Iteration Paths as scoping containers — how they intersect with the Epic→Task hierarchy (orthogonal axis), how permissions/visibility are scoped against them, whether they form their own tree, and how they differ semantically from the work-item parent chain; (10) Project as a scoping container — what a "Project" means in Azure DevOps and what crosses Project boundaries.
summary: Azure DevOps models Epic→Feature→Story→Task as one work-item store linked by tree-topology Parent links, with Area/Iteration Paths and Projects as orthogonal scopes.
content:
Contents
  1. Product Overview
  2. Hierarchy & Schema (Topics 1–4)
  3. Mutation & Lifecycle (Topics 5–6)
  4. Filtering & Customization (Topics 7–8)
  5. Scoping Containers (Topics 9–10)
  6. Comparison Notes
  7. Sources
  8. Gaps & Limitations

Product Overview

Azure Boards is the work-tracking subsystem of Azure DevOps (cloud Services and on-prem Server). Each project is created from one of four built-in process templates — Basic, Agile, Scrum, or CMMI — which determine the available work item types and their default hierarchy [1]. The Basic process ships only Epics, Issues, and Tasks; Agile uses Epic / Feature / User Story / Task plus Bug; Scrum uses Epic / Feature / Product Backlog Item / Task plus Bug; CMMI uses Epic / Feature / Requirement / Task plus Bug, Change Request, Risk, Issue, and Review [1][2]. Access tiers are Stakeholder (limited), Basic (full work-tracking), and Basic + Test Plans; Stakeholder users in public projects get the same work-tracking surface as Basic users [1]. The Basic process is only available on Azure DevOps Services and on Azure DevOps Server 2020 and later; older on-prem deployments use Agile, Scrum, or CMMI [1].

Hierarchy & Schema (Topics 1–4)

1. Schema / data model

All work items live in a single logical work-item store regardless of type: “Each work item is an object stored in the work item data store and receives a unique identifier within an organization or project collection” [1]. The type is a field on the work item, not a separate table — “Each work item uses a work item type that determines which fields are available for tracking information” [1]. The same Change-type action can switch a saved work item from Bug to Task, refreshing the form to the new type’s field set without changing the ID, which only makes sense if all types share one underlying record [3]. Categories (Epic, Feature, Requirement, Task, Bug) group types and decide which backlog and board they appear on, so a custom WIT joining the Requirement Category will surface on the product backlog without code changes [1].

Parent–child links are stored as link records, not as a parent_id column. The system‐defined hierarchy link is System.LinkTypes.Hierarchy with a Forward (Child) and Reverse (Parent) end, topology tree, and is one of many link types stored uniformly in the link store [4]. The CLI / REST endpoint az boards work-item relation list-type returns hierarchy, related, dependency, duplicate, tested-by, and remote relationships from the same relation registry [4].

2. Strategy↔execution boundary

Azure separates portfolio (strategy) work from product/iteration (execution) work via backlog levels rather than a hard table boundary. Out of the box there are two portfolio backlogs — Epics and Features — above the Requirement-category backlog (Stories / PBIs / Requirements / Issues), and a separate Iteration backlog (Tasks) [5][6]. “Portfolio backlogs let you add and group items into a hierarchy… minimize size variability of your deliverables by breaking down a large feature into smaller backlog items” [5]. The seam is soft: the same store, the same link types, just different categories that the UI surfaces on different backlog pages. “Features roll up into epics” and “user stories or backlog items roll up into features” is a UI/category convention, not a foreign-key constraint [5].

3. Parent–child link rules

Parent-Child is a directional tree-topology link with strict cardinality: “A work item can have only one Parent. A parent work item can have many children” [4]. Tree links are also acyclic — “prevents circular references between work items” [4]. Critically, the tree link is one of many link types and is distinct from peer relationships:

  • Tree topology, system-defined: Parent–Child (System.LinkTypes.Hierarchy), Duplicate–Duplicate Of [4].
  • Network topology, system-defined: Related (System.LinkTypes.Related) — nondirectional, few restrictions, used for peer relationships [4].
  • Dependency topology, system-defined: Successor–Predecessor (System.LinkTypes.Dependency) — directional, acyclic, but not a tree (one item can have many predecessors and many successors) [4].
  • Dependency topology, process-defined: Tested By–Tests, Affects–Affected By (CMMI), Test Case–Shared Steps, Referenced By–References [4].
  • Cross-organization: Remote Related and Consumes From–Produces For for Microsoft-Entra-linked organizations [4].
  • External / hyperlink: Branch, Commit, Pull Request, Build, Storyboard, Wiki, Tag, Test Result, plus arbitrary URL hyperlinks [4].

Per-link-type counters (Related Link Count, External Link Count, Hyperlink Count, Remote Link Count) are maintained as fields on each work item [4].

4. Rollup mechanics

Rollup is computed by the Analytics service, not maintained by triggers on the work-item table. Microsoft is explicit: “Rollup data is calculated from the Analytics service. Large datasets may cause display latency” [7]. On-prem deployments must have the Analytics extension installed for rollup columns to work at all [7]. The UI shows a status icon on the rollup cell and a transient “info”/empty-row state when Analytics is still processing recent edits, and “Rollup columns refresh automatically once processing completes” [7] — i.e., eventually consistent, computed on read against the Analytics projection.

What can be rolled up: progress bars (% of descendant items closed/completed), counts of descendant items, and sums of numeric fields (Effort, Story Points, Completed Work, Remaining Work) [5][7]. Custom WITs and custom numeric fields can also be rolled up via Column Options → Configure custom rollup [7]. Important constraints: “Rollup supports progress bars, work item counts, and numeric field sums within a project only… Cross-project child items and test case links aren’t included in rollup calculations… Rollup of Effort, Story Points, or Size fields isn’t supported across product and portfolio backlogs” [7]. The fields used by capacity, sprint burndown, velocity, and forecast (Remaining Work, Story Points / Effort / Size, Activity / Discipline) are configured at the process level [3][6].

Mutation & Lifecycle (Topics 5–6)

5. Reparenting

Reparenting is a first-class, supported operation. From any backlog with the Parents view enabled, “you can reparent items by dragging a child item to a new parent”, or use the Mapping pane, or multi-select and use Change parent… [8]. Reparenting respects category rules: “You can only reparent backlog items under other features, and features under other epics” [8]. The reparent rewrites the parent link; no work-item ID changes; the History tab records the link change like any other field edit [1][8]. Because rollup is recomputed by Analytics from the current link graph rather than stored on the parent, moving a User Story between Features causes Analytics to re-attribute story points / counts / progress to the new parent on the next refresh — there is no manual rebuild step. Caveat: historical trend / Analytics snapshot reports use the work item’s historical Area/Iteration values, so historical Power BI snapshots may continue to show the story under its old grouping until the snapshot point [9]. Stakeholder users on private projects can’t drag-reparent [8].

6. Orphan handling

Orphans are allowed and surfaced, never blocked. The backlog explicitly lists “Unparented backlog items” at the end of the parented set when the Parents view is on, exactly so they can be mapped to a parent later [8]. AI-mode prompts and built-in queries are documented for the workflow “List all user stories that don’t have a parent feature assigned” [8]. Categories also dictate where types appear: a Bug, by team setting, may be tracked at the User-Story level (on the product backlog) or at the Task level (under a story); either way, an unparented Bug is permitted [1][6]. There is no global “must have parent” rule and no documented warning when saving a parentless item.

Filtering & Customization (Topics 7–8)

7. Subtree filtering

“Show me everything under this Feature” is supported four different ways:

  • Hierarchical backlog view: turn on Parents in View options; the backlog renders the tree and Expand/Collapse icons walk the hierarchy [8].
  • Mapping pane: select a parent and see all child candidates [8].
  • Tree-of-work-items query: WIQL queries can use the Parent–Child link type as a tree query to return a parent and all descendants in a single result [4][1].
  • Area Path filter: if a Feature’s subtree shares an Area Path, the orthogonal Area-Path scope captures it (see Topic 9) — but Microsoft warns that area paths and parent links can drift, and offers MCP-prompt audits to find children whose Area Path differs from the parent’s [8].

Cross-project tree queries are not supported: “Can I query for work items linked across projects? No. There’s a prohibitive performance cost for trying to execute such a query, so it isn’t supported” [10].

8. Custom level naming and inherited process

Customization is governed by the process model: Inheritance (cloud + AzDO Server 2019/2020+), Hosted XML (legacy cloud), or On-premises XML (Server) [3]. Inheritance is WYSIWYG and is the recommended path. Limits on hierarchy customization are explicit:

  • Up to 5 portfolio backlog levels total above the requirement backlog [6]. The default two are Epics and Features; you can add Initiatives, Strategies, etc. as additional top levels.
  • “You can’t insert a new custom backlog level within the existing set of defined backlogs” — e.g., you cannot inject “Initiative” between Epic and Feature; new portfolio levels stack on top of Epics [6].
  • “You can’t reorder the backlog levels” and “You can’t add a WIT to two different backlog levels” [6].
  • You can rename any backlog level, including renaming the requirement backlog (e.g., “Stories and Tickets”), and you can rename Epic/Feature [6].
  • You can’t remove an inherited portfolio level, but you can disable its default WIT once a custom WIT is added in its place [6].
  • Inheritance does not allow creation of custom link types — that is XML-process-only [3].

When you add a WIT to a backlog level, the system silently injects required hidden fields (Stack Rank / Backlog Priority on portfolios; Story Points / Size / Effort on the requirement backlog; Activity / Remaining Work on iteration) so velocity, burndown, and capacity tools keep working [6]. Custom processes inherit from a system process, and when the parent process changes, all child projects pick up the change automatically [3].

Scoping Containers (Topics 9–10)

9. Area Paths and Iteration Paths

Area Paths and Iteration Paths are orthogonal trees to the Epic→Task parent chain. “Area paths group work items by team, product, or feature area. Iteration paths group work into sprints, milestones, or other time-related periods. Both fields support hierarchical paths” [11]. Internally they are Classification Nodes managed via the Classification Nodes REST API, and stored as the Area Path and Iteration Path fields on each work item with the TreePath data type (backslash-separated, max 14 levels deep, max 4000 chars, max 10,000 nodes per project, max 300 paths per team) [11].

Key distinctions versus the work-item parent chain:

  • Permissions are scoped to Area Path nodes (not to parent links). The two core ACLs are “View work items in this node” and “Edit work items in this node”, with finer-grained “Edit work item comments in this node”, etc. [1][11]. There is no equivalent permission keyed to a parent Epic.
  • Team ownership binds a team to one or more Area Paths; a team’s backlog/board is a filtered view of work items at the team’s assigned Area Path(s) [11]. Multiple teams can share an Area Path but Microsoft warns this can cause ownership conflicts [11].
  • Iteration is purely a planning grouping: “Iterations don’t enforce any rules” — you can leave a task active past its iteration end [11]. Sprint burndown and velocity are keyed off iteration.
  • Reporting hazards: “Deleting Area Path values or reconfiguring Iteration Path values causes irreversible data loss” in burndown widgets, sprint burndown, velocity charts, and historical trend charts that reference the path values “as defined at the time for each work item” [11].
  • Independence from parent: a child work item’s Area Path does not have to match the parent’s; built-in audit prompts exist to find children whose Area Path differs from the parent’s [8]. The two trees coexist without a propagation rule.

10. Project as a scoping container

A Project in Azure DevOps is the top-level container for work items, repos, pipelines, boards, queries, area/iteration paths, and process inheritance. Microsoft’s recommendation is “a single project” for simplicity, with multiple projects only when business needs (public-vs-private split, segregated security policies) require it [10]. What does and does not cross project boundaries:

  • Crosses project boundaries: cross-project queries (“You can create work item queries that span all projects within an organization”), cross-project work-item links of any link type, Power BI / Analytics reports across projects, and cross-project search [10].
  • Does not cross project boundaries: rollup calculations (rollup is within-project only) [7]; cross-project tree queries (blocked for performance) [10]; capacity planning (per-project only) [10]; inherited process customization is project-collection scoped, but you can export and re-import processes between organizations using the Process Migrator tool [10].
  • Cross-organization linking exists in cloud only via the Remote Related / Consumes From–Produces For link types, and only when both organizations are bound to the same Microsoft Entra tenant [4][10]. On Azure DevOps Server, cross-collection links are not supported [10].
  • Move work item to another project: supported within an organization/collection. The work-item ID is preserved, full revision history is preserved, but “Child work items don’t get moved and remain in the origin project, but the Parent-Child links remain in place” — i.e., you can end up with cross-project parent–child links after a move [3]. Test-management types and Hidden Types Category items cannot be moved or have their type changed [3].

Comparison Notes

The Microsoft docs do not contain a direct apples-to-apples comparison with Jira or Rally. Implicit contrasts surfacing in the docs:

  • Unlike Jira’s Epic–Story relationship (historically a custom field on the Story pointing to the Epic), Azure uses a generic Parent–Child link type that the same backlog UI uses for Feature→Story and Story→Task — the levels are the convention, the link mechanism is uniform [4][5].
  • Unlike Jira’s sub-task being a special work item type with restricted parent options, Azure Tasks are full work items with the same form mechanics, just associated with the Iteration backlog category [1][6].
  • Unlike Jira’s Project = container of issues, an Azure DevOps Project also bundles repos, pipelines, wikis, dashboards, and security groups, so the multi-project decision is not just a backlog-partition decision [10].
  • Sprint mechanics are stored on a separate Iteration Path tree rather than a sprint object dangling off a board, which means iterations are reusable across teams and queryable as fields [11].

Sources

  1. About work items and work item types - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/work-items/about-work-items?view=azure-devops (last updated 2026-02-10, ms.date 2025-10-08)
  2. Agile Workflow in Azure Boards - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/work-items/guidance/agile-process-workflow?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2025-07-30)
  3. Customize your work tracking experience - Azure DevOps, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/reference/customize-work?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2026-02-17)
  4. Link Types Reference Guide - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/queries/link-type-reference?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2025-10-08, last updated 2025-12-19)
  5. Define features and epics to organize backlog items - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/backlogs/define-features-epics?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2026-02-28)
  6. Customize Backlogs and Boards - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/organizations/settings/work/customize-process-backlogs-boards?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2025-08-20)
  7. Display rollup columns to show progress, counts, or totals in Azure Boards - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/backlogs/display-rollup?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2025-07-01)
  8. Tutorial: Organize Your Product Backlog in Azure Boards - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/backlogs/organize-backlog?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2026-02-28)
  9. Rollup child work item values to sample report - Azure DevOps, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/report/powerbi/sample-boards-rollup?view=azure-devops
  10. Cross-project FAQs - Azure DevOps, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/project/work-across-projects-faqs?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2026-04-13)
  11. How are area and iteration paths used? - Azure DevOps, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/organizations/settings/about-areas-iterations?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2025-12-22)
  12. Move work items and change the work item type in Azure Boards - Azure Boards, Microsoft Learn. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/devops/boards/backlogs/move-change-type?view=azure-devops (ms.date 2026-02-28)

Gaps & Limitations

  • Microsoft’s public docs do not expose the physical schema of the work-item store (table names, indexes, link table layout); all schema claims here are inferred from observable behavior, the link-type CLI output, and the REST surface. The product is closed-source and the SQL schema is not part of the public contract.
  • The exact freshness SLO of Analytics rollup (“processing recent changes”) is described qualitatively but not as a numeric latency target in the cited pages [7].
  • Whether reparenting a User Story rewrites historical Analytics snapshots, or only the live projection, is implied by Analytics’ historical-snapshot model but not stated as an explicit guarantee on the rollup page [7][9]. Power BI samples for historical rollup were noted in search results but not separately deep-fetched.
  • The cross-organization Remote Related / Consumes From–Produces For link types are documented for cloud only; the equivalent on-prem cross-collection story is not explicitly covered beyond “not supported” [4][10].
  • Process-template differences (Agile vs Scrum vs CMMI vs Basic) are documented at the WIT-name level but the docs reviewed here do not give an exhaustive field-level diff; for that the per-process workflow articles (e.g. cmmi-process-workflow) would need to be fetched separately.
  • The Hosted XML process model and on-prem-only customizations (custom link types, global lists, witadmin field reassignment) are mentioned but not deeply characterized; readers needing those should consult the on-premises XML reference set [3].
